evals.prompt: I'm on branch feature/auth-jwt. I've committed the JWT authentication implementation. Run /gitf.
evals.expected_output: Surveys facts (gitf-survey.sh): topic branch, ahead_of_develop>0 → FLOW A. Pushes feature/auth-jwt, creates PR targeting develop, checks mergeStateStatus, merges with merge commit, syncs develop, runs CLEANUP. Reports which branch was merged and that develop is now up to date.

evals.prompt: I'm on the develop branch. I've been committing directly to develop by mistake — there are 3 commits here that should be on a feature branch. The commits are about adding a dark mode toggle to the settings page. Run /gitf.
evals.expected_output: Survey reports is_develop=true with commits ahead of origin → routes to flow-d → flow-a. Creates a new branch named something like feature/settings-dark-mode based on commit messages. Moves the commits there using reset --hard on develop and checking out the new branch. Then proceeds with FLOW A: PR to develop, merge, sync.

evals.prompt: I'm on develop. The branch is clean (no uncommitted changes). develop has 5 commits that aren't in main yet — a new search feature and a couple of bug fixes. The current version is 1.3.2. Run /gitf -v.
evals.expected_output: Survey reports is_develop=true, develop_ahead_of_main>0 → FLOW B. Determines version bump (minor, because there's a new feature) → new version is 1.4.0. Creates release/v1.4.0 from develop. Bumps version in the version file. Checks mergeStateStatus before merging. PRs release to main, merges it. Tags v1.4.0 on main. PRs release to develop (back-merge), merges it. Deletes release branch local and remote. Syncs main and develop. Reports the full release summary.

evals.prompt: I'm on feature/payment-stripe. I previously ran /gitf and it created PR #7 to develop, but the repo requires a reviewer before merge, so /gitf reported the PR blocked and stopped. A teammate approved and the PR was merged on GitHub. I run /gitf again.
evals.expected_output: No state file is consulted. Survey reports merged_into_develop=true with the branch still present (the prior run never cleaned up) → FLOW A cleanup-only path. The github provider re-locates the PR via gh pr list --head feature/payment-stripe --base develop --state all, sees state=MERGED, syncs develop, and runs CLEANUP (delete branch, remove any worktree). Reports the flow is complete and develop is up to date. Position is derived from the graph + gh, never from a stored entry.

evals.prompt: I'm on release/v2.1.1. develop had 3 bug-fix commits ahead of main (was version 2.1.0). I previously ran /gitf -v; it created release/v2.1.1, bumped the version, and opened PR #9 to main, but required status checks were still running, so /gitf reported it blocked and stopped. CI has now passed and PR #9 was merged. I run /gitf.
evals.expected_output: Survey reports gitf_branch=release → FLOW B (continue release). No state exists, so version_mode/version are inferred from the branch name: release/v2.1.1 → version_mode=true, version=2.1.1. The B-5 land-to-main step re-locates the PR via gh pr list --head release/v2.1.1 --base main --state all, sees MERGED → advances. Syncs main, creates annotated tag v2.1.1, pushes the tag. Creates the back-merge PR (release/v2.1.1 → develop, with --head). Checks mergeStateStatus; if CLEAN, merges, syncs develop, CLEANUP deletes the release branch local and remote (and any worktree). Reports the full release summary.

evals.prompt: I have two topic branches with open work: feature/auth-jwt (PR #3 to develop, still open) and feature/search (PR #5 to develop). I'm currently checked out on feature/search and PR #5 was just merged on GitHub. I run /gitf.
evals.expected_output: Each branch's status is independent and derived from its own graph position — there is no shared state file. The survey of feature/search reports merged_into_develop=true with the branch still present → FLOW A cleanup-only. The github provider re-locates PR #5 via gh pr list --head feature/search, confirms MERGED, syncs develop, and runs CLEANUP on feature/search only. feature/auth-jwt is never touched: it is a different branch and would be handled on its own /gitf run when checked out. Reports feature/search done.

evals.prompt: I'm on a topic branch called issue-2087-login (no feature/ or fix/ prefix) with fresh commits ahead of develop. I run /gitf.
evals.expected_output: Routing is by topology, never by name prefix. The survey reports a topic branch (not develop/main/release/hotfix) with ahead_of_develop>0 → FLOW A. On the github provider the LAND step first probes for an existing PR (gh pr list --head issue-2087-login --base develop --state all) before creating one, then lands idempotently: pushes the branch, opens a PR to develop, checks mergeStateStatus, merges, syncs develop, CLEANUP. The unconventional branch name is treated exactly like any other topic branch.

evals.prompt: I'm on release/v1.4.0. The PR from release/v1.4.0 to main was already created and merged, the tag v1.4.0 already exists on main, but the back-merge PR to develop was never made (the previous run stopped). There is no .gitf/state.json anywhere. I run /gitf.
evals.expected_output: FLOW B with position re-derived entirely from the git graph and gh — no saved state. version_mode/version are inferred from the branch name (release/v1.4.0 → version_mode=true, version=1.4.0). The version-bump probe sees the version file already at 1.4.0 → skips B-3. The land-to-main probe (gh pr list --head release/v1.4.0 --base main --state all) finds the PR already MERGED → skips creating/merging it. The tag probe (git tag -l v1.4.0) is non-empty → skips TAG. Proceeds to the back-merge step, probes for that PR (none) → creates the release→develop PR and lands it, then cleans up. Never double-creates a PR, double-merges, or double-tags.

evals.prompt: I'm on develop, clean, with commits ahead of main, ready to cut a release. But there is also a hotfix/login-crash branch that has commits not yet merged into main (an urgent fix still in progress). I run /gitf.
evals.expected_output: Survey routes to FLOW B (is_develop, develop_ahead_of_main>0). The B-0 in-flight guard probes 'release/*' and 'hotfix/*' for branches with commits not in main and finds hotfix/login-crash. It HALTS: tells the user a release must wait for the unfinished hotfix, and to finish or delete hotfix/login-crash before starting the release. It does NOT create a release branch and does NOT append -2. (A release waits for any in-flight production change.) The guard is derived from git branches, not stored state.

evals.prompt: A release is mid-flight: release/v3.0.0 exists with commits not yet in main. An urgent production bug comes in, so I create hotfix/cart-total off main, commit the fix, and run /gitf while on hotfix/cart-total.
evals.expected_output: Survey reports gitf_branch=hotfix → FLOW C. The C-0 in-flight guard probes only OTHER 'hotfix/*' branches (excluding the current one) — it deliberately does NOT probe release/*, because a hotfix is highest priority and does not wait for an in-flight release. No other hotfix exists, so it does NOT halt. The hotfix proceeds: lands to main, tags the patch version, back-merges to develop, cleans up. release/v3.0.0 is left untouched and is handled on its own later /gitf run (which will then wait correctly via B-0 because the hotfix is gone).

evals.prompt: I'm on release/v1.5.0. A previous /gitf ran the code-review gate, which found issues needing my decision and stopped. I've since addressed the feedback and committed the changes to the release branch. I run /gitf.
evals.expected_output: Survey reports gitf_branch=release → FLOW B. No state is read. Routing lands back on release/v1.5.0 and re-enters the code-review gate from the top (idempotent), detecting reviewers live and re-running them on main..release/v1.5.0. If the gate now passes, it continues FLOW B from B-5 (land release → main). If the gate still reports unresolved findings, it emits blocked-code-review and halts again — re-runnable with no stored state.

evals.prompt: I'm working in a linked git worktree at ../wt-featx that holds the branch feat-x (created with git worktree add). feat-x has commits ahead of develop, and develop is checked out in the main worktree. I run /gitf from inside the linked worktree.
evals.expected_output: Survey reports current_is_linked=true, current_path=the linked worktree, develop_at=the main worktree → FLOW A on topic branch feat-x. LAND merges feat-x into develop inside develop's own worktree (git -C <develop_at> merge --no-ff) — it never runs 'git checkout develop' in the linked worktree. After landing, CLEANUP steps out to main_path, removes the feat-x worktree with 'git worktree remove' (no --force), deletes the feat-x branch, and prunes. Reports feat-x landed and the worktree removed.

evals.prompt: Same setup as before: feat-y lives in a linked worktree and has just been merged into develop. But that worktree has an uncommitted/untracked file in it. I run /gitf.
evals.expected_output: CLEANUP tries 'git worktree remove' WITHOUT --force. git refuses because the worktree is not clean — that refusal is the intended guard. The flow prints GITF_HALT and STOPS, telling the user the worktree at <path> has uncommitted/untracked changes and must be cleaned or removed by hand. It does NOT pass --force and does NOT delete the branch out from under the dirty worktree.

evals.prompt: I'm on develop in a pure-local repo with no remote configured (no origin, no gh). develop has a feature commit ahead of main. I run /gitf -v.
evals.expected_output: Survey reports platform.provider=local, has_remote=false → FLOW B with the local provider. All LAND steps are synchronous --no-ff merges (no PRs); push/pull are no-ops. If develop is checked out where the merge target lives, the merge runs in develop's worktree; if a target branch is not checked out anywhere, an ephemeral worktree is created for the merge and removed afterward. Bumps version, merges release into main, tags, back-merges into develop, deletes the release branch, reports the release summary. Never blocks.
